An angry mob of 200 Black residents blocked streets and gathered at the United States marshal's office to protest the alleged assault of a 14-year-old Black girl by a white grocery store employee.
Location: Muskogee, Indian Territory Participants: Approximately 200 Black residents Event date: August 4, 1907
Source text
Muskogee, I. T., Aug. 4.—Declaring that one of the white employes of a grocery had assaulted Anna Allen, a Negro girl, an angry mob of 200 Negroes blocked one of the streets here to day and hung about
 Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1907-08-24 mass_meeting
The Niagara Movement held a notable meeting to address grievances related to Roosevelt's policies toward African Americans.
Location: National Capital Participants: Niagara Movement members Event date: 1907-08-24
Source text
Social Inactivity at the National Capital-Over 10,000 Negroes were Present at "Negro Day" at Jamestown; "Physicians' Day" will be August 30th-Niagara Movement Hold Notable Meeting-Prof. Kelley Miller
Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1908-02-22 other
An anti-Jim Crow league was organized to raise funds and push legal action against racial segregation laws.
Location: nan Participants: Members of an anti-Jim Crow league; at least two donors contributing $100 each Event date: nan
Source text
have organized an anti-Jim Crow league and are raising funds to push the fight to a conclusion. Two men contributed $100 each and others from $1 up. The money will be raised. The Kansas City Star say

1908-03-07 demonstration
The Boston Massacre of March 5, 1770, where armed soldiers confronted citizens including the Black man Crispus Attucks, resulting in the first bloodshed of the American Revolution.
Location: Boston Participants: Citizens armed with clubs, British soldiers, Crispus Attucks (Negro slave and recognized leader) Event date: March 5, 1770
Source text
[March 5, 1770, in the streets of Boston, was shed the first blood for American Independence and among the first to fall was the Negro slave, Attucks, the recognized leader of the citizens.] They wer

p. 1
1908-03-14 petition
A formal petition or statement demanding equal rights, justice, and fair treatment for African Americans in political representation and industrial opportunities.
Location: nan Participants: African Americans Event date: nan
Source text
to life, liberty and the pursuit of happiness. The same, no more, no less than white men possess; and to a voice in the government, state and national, exactly as other citizens enjoy. We do not ask a

1908-03-14 petition
An appeal to authorities requesting legislative action to remove Jim Crow car laws.
Location: nan Participants: Liberty-loving men in authority Event date: nan

1908-03-14 petition
A collective appeal to the Supreme Court, Southern State legislatures, and Congress demanding the repeal of discriminatory laws and restoration of constitutional rights denied to African Americans.
Location: nan Participants: Leaders of the African American community, bishops of the church, and members of the race Event date: nan

best of our race, from the bishops of the church to the humblest, while at the same time we are required to pay the same fare for inferior accommodation. We appeal to the judges of the Supreme Court t

1908-03-28 delegation
Oklahoma Negroes filed a legal application in federal court seeking a court order to restrain enforcement of the state Jim Crow law and challenge its constitutionality.
Location: Oklahoma City, Oklahoma Participants: Oklahoma Negroes (legal complainants) Event date: March 24, 1908
Source text

Hotel and restaurant keepers' association agreed to pay for Colored Elks event at St. Paul auditorium in exchange for reception committees instructing visitors to avoid establishments displaying discriminatory signs.

1908-08-29 demonstration
Audience members hissed a speaker off the platform in response to his inflammatory racist remarks at a packed courthouse gathering.
Location: courthouse Participants: Audience members at a packed courthouse Event date: 1908-08-29
Source text
platform. He said that the Democratic party was the only "white man's party," and that any Republican who would not vote for those two planks should live to see the day when "his daughter would wed a

1908-08-29 demonstration
Rev. W. P. Dorsey was hooted and disrupted while speaking against disfranchisement and Jim Crow provisions at a Democratic convention.

p. 1
1908-09-12 boycott
Neighborhood residents, both Black and white, organized a successful boycott against a Southern restaurant owner who posted a sign refusing Black patronage, forcing the business to close.

R. R. Wright organized Negro mechanics in Philadelphia to collectively demand equal pay and work opportunities with white laborers.

p. 1
1908-11-21 delegation
Dr. Robert W. Brown wrote a letter to the President protesting racial discrimination in the lunch room of Union Station, asserting the grievance on behalf of 90,000 colored people in Washington D.C. and all colored people in the country.

A strike on the Georgia Railroad against the employment of Negro firemen, aimed at restricting job opportunities for Black workers in favor of white labor.

were no separate schools, churches, cars and public houses, to mark the son of Ham as distinct and cursed. The black was a part of the people in every way. With the close of the Spanish-American war a

1912-07-20 mass_meeting
Members of the National Independent Political League held a meeting to protest the organization's support of Colonel Roosevelt and to reorganize around opposition to both Roosevelt and Taft.
Location: Philadelphia, Pennsylvania (Zion Baptist church, Thirteenth and Wallace streets, and 1234 Melon St.) Participants: Officers and delegates of the National Independent Political League (composed of colored men) Event date: July 6, 1912
Source text
Philadephia, July 6. 1912. —The convention of the National Independent Political League, which is composed of Colored men, broke up yesterday afternoon with the withdrawal of nearly all the officers w

p. 1
1912-07-27 delegation
A group of colored people attempted to present a committee to the Democratic convention in Baltimore to advocate for inclusion of Negro rights in the party platform.
Location: Baltimore Participants: Active and strong group of colored people Event date: 1912 (Democratic convention)
Source text
Further than this, it is known that an active and strong group of colored people hung around the Democratic convention in Baltimore seeking to get the Democrats to put a line or two in their platform

p. 1
1912-08-31 delegation
Colored delegates appealed to Roosevelt at the convention after being expelled, demanding justice for their exclusion.

Source text

p. 1

The Negro Outlook Committee petitioned the Park Board to equip a lot and provide additional playground facilities for colored children.

Source text

this case proceedings will be started immediately in every other State where similar statutes exist. Among the membership of the Knights of Moses are two thousand Negro ministers and bishops, and they

A collective action by colored people to withdraw their business and deposits from Louisville Trust Company in protest of their dismissal of Negro janitors and employment of Greek workers instead.

Source text

A committee of five white persons and five Negroes issued a statement protesting against a proposed segregation ordinance in St. Louis.

Source text

Colored citizens of Roswell protested the appointment of a teacher of questionable reputation by refusing to send their children to the school.

1913-06-14 delegation
Italian representatives notified the Italian embassy and the State Department to protest racial and ethnic discrimination in real estate sales in Newark, N.J.

Source text
Nework, N. J., June 6. When a prominent addition to this city was placed on the market not long since, the announcement was made that the property would not be sold to colored people nor to Italians.

African Americans are discouraging the establishment of a segregated library for colored people, advocating instead for equal access to existing library facilities used by white people.

1913-07-19 strike
Strikers, primarily mine owners' employees, rioted in the business section and attacked the Rand Club, resulting in violent confrontation with dragoons and troops.
Location: Rand Club in the business section (appears to be in a mining region) Participants: Strikers, mine owners, dragoons, troops, armed citizens Event date: 1913-07-19
Source text
The mobs in the business section caused less fear than the desperados threatening to dynamite the homes of the wealthy in the outskirts. All places of business were closed and the troops were depended

1913-07-19 strike
Black workers adopted red rosettes in imitation of white strikers demanding higher pay, with strike leaders attempting to negotiate terms amid worker dissatisfaction.
Location: Johannesburg and Pretoria Participants: Black workers, white strikers, strike leaders Event date: 1913-07-19
Source text
Most of the blacks have donned red rosettes in imitation of the white strikers, who they believe struck for higher pay and obtained it. The natives argue that they can do the same thing. Most of the

1913-07-19 strike
Six thousand Black laborers from gold mines on the Rand refused to work until granted wage increases, with 1,000 breaking out of their compound before being forced back by military troops.

Native African workers in South Africa struck for better conditions against oppression and persecution by Dutch, Belgian, and English authorities, with fighting resulting in approximately 110 casualties.

African Americans in New York plan to boycott an October exposition commemorating fifty years of freedom unless the governor removes or redirects nine Negro commissioners appointed to oversee the event.

A community boycott of Luna Park's segregated amusement facilities and the emancipation celebration and picnic events held there, with the African American community refusing to attend in protest of discriminatory treatment.

1913-10-04 demonstration

schools so panic stricken at one small voice? Why is the American Negro hater always so anxious to affirm that the Negro assents to his chains and insults, or that the "responsible" Negroes assent or

1913-10-11 petition
Citizens sent strong memorials to the White House protesting racial segregation policies enforced by cabinet officials in federal administrative departments.

Source text
Has the president yet given adequate consideration to the new policy of race segregation enforced in certain of the great administrative departments at Washington? This question must be his to decide,

1913-10-11 demonstration

Source text

1916-03-18 other
African American ministers and the ministerial board withdrew cooperation with white ministers in response to racial discrimination (Jim Crow treatment) at Billy Sunday's tabernacle revival.
Location: Denver Participants: Rev. S. A. Virgil, Rev. E. S. Williams, and the ministerial board members Event date: 1916-03-18
Source text
ministers, Rev. S. A. Virgil and Rev, E. S. Williams, and three other friends went to the tabernacle to hear Billy Sunday preach. An usher ap proached the party and informed them that they would have

p. 1
1916-04-15 delegation
African Americans of Denver protested against the showing of 'The Birth of a Nation' and appealed to city official Mr. Nisbet, culminating in a large meeting in the council chamber where Nisbet voted against their demands.
Location: Denver Participants: Negroes of Denver (as described in text), Mr. Nisbet, council members Event date: nan
Source text
It was Mr. Nisbet who had the SOLE POWER to prohibit the infamous photo-drama "The Birth of a Nation" from showing in Denver. The Negroes of this city, as a unit, protested against the portrayal of th
 Denver Star
p. 1
1916-05-13 demonstration
African American voters in Denver conducted a coordinated voting demonstration on Tuesday to oppose the film 'Birth of a Nation' and resist discriminatory treatment by voting as a united bloc for Mayor Robert W. Speer.
Location: Denver Participants: Negro men and women voters across the city, particularly in Districts C, H, and T Event date: 1916-05-09 (Tuesday of the week of May 13)
Source text
Every Negro, true to his race and honor as a man and citizen and conscious of the vital importance of victory in this death grappling struggle for better consideration from his fellow citizens with sh

p. 1
1916-05-27 other
Dr. Ransom calls for Black Americans to demand the establishment of a separate Colored regiment with Colored officers as a condition for military service.
Location: nan Participants: Black Americans (described as 10,000,000 strong) Event date: nan
Source text

p. 1
1916-06-17 delegation
A commission of representative Negro leaders presented their grievances to the Louisiana Railroad Commission demanding better accommodations for Black passengers on state railroads.
Location: Baton Rouge, Louisiana Participants: Representative Negroes of Louisiana Event date: Recently (before June 17, 1916)
Source text

1916-07-22 other
The N.A.A.C.P. launched a fundraising campaign to raise $10,000 to protest and publicize the lynching of Jesse Washington in Waco, Texas, including distribution of 50,000 copies documenting the lynching.

Source text

Negro women organized clubs to demand political recognition and protest their exclusion from political positions despite representing 7000 votes.

Negro citizens of Westfield organized collective action in response to a colored woman being held in virtual slavery and denied police protection.

Source text

1916-08-19 parade
A preparedness parade held in June where someone circulated printed protest cards signed 'Justice' objecting to discriminatory treatment of Black federal employees.

President, and seems to be able to put anything over on the "black man." This last order of his affects about four hundred colored employees, among which are about thirty clerks. The disgraceful thing

Colored people arose en masse and marched to police headquarters to protest police brutality against a woman and the striking of Mrs. Agnes Stewart by an officer.

Source text
This National Colored Citizenship Rights Congress is to be an open one, both to delegated persons and to individual representatives of the cause who attend on their own responsibility It is to be free

p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
General Electric plant workers walked out and paraded through the streets to Crescent Park in a strike action.
Location: General Electric plant, River Road, Dock Street, State Street, Crescent Park Participants: General Electric plant workers/strikers Event date: 1917-06-30
Source text
plant was practically suspended, as the men walked out, everybody gathering to see the parade. "George E. Emmons, general manager of the plant, watched the men leave the plant from his office window.
 Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
Machinists' organizations in Schenectady went on strike against General Electric officials for refusing to allow workers to present grievances through a committee.
Location: Schenectady Participants: Machinists' organizations Event date: nan
Source text

p. 1
1917-06-30 delegation
A committee of machinists presented a demand to Mr. Emmons to remove a young colored college student from his job due to his race.
Location: nan Participants: Committee of machinists, Mr. Emmons, young colored college student Event date: nan
Source text
Mr. Emmons contends a committee of the machinists practically demanded that the young colored man be taken off that work to which Mr. Emmons replied that he could see no reason, for establishing a col

p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
A strike by the machinists' organization against the employment of a Negro worker in their shop, demanding racial segregation of workers.
Location: nan Participants: Machinists' organization, Mr. Lefkowitz (speaker) Event date: nan
Source text

Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
Machinists at a General Electric plant in Schenectady walked out in protest against discriminatory color line policies, organized by union officials with an orderly parade.
Location: Schenectady, G.E. Plant Participants: Machinists, union officials, young girls Event date: 1917 (9:15 AM timing mentioned)
Source text
The Schenectady Union-Star, in giving an account of the strike in its news columns under the headline, "Machinists Demand Same Color Line in G. E. Plant as Exists in U. S. Army," gives the following f
 Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
Machinists walked out and delivered an ultimatum demanding recognition of their committee and opposition to employment of Negro labor.
Location: Colorado (Denver area implied by Colorado Statesman) Participants: Machinists union members including Joseph Lefkowitz, William Hogan, W. L. Sargent, W. Walker, and E. Cooley Event date: 1917-06-30 (Friday night union meeting decided ultimatum; Tuesday night walk-out)
Source text
Mr. Emmons left his office last Tuesday night when he received the ultimatum of the union. The ultimatum was decided on at a meeting of the union Friday night and said in part: "We take this means of
Colorado Statesman
p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
2,500 machinists and toolmakers at General Electric Company went on strike demanding racial segregation policies similar to those practiced in the U.S. Army.
Location: Denver Participants: 2,500 machinists and toolmakers at General Electric Company Event date: Monday (June 25, 1917)
Source text
BY MANAGER G. E. EMMONS. "It is contrary to the policy of the company to take any action detrimental to the best interests of its employees, but it will tolerate no discrimination against any worthy

p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
A strike event with reported incidents connected to it.
Location: Denver Participants: nan Event date: 1917-06-30
Source text
Incidents in Connection With Strike.

p. 1
1917-06-30 strike
Union workers at General Electric Company's machine shops went on strike in response to a colored worker being employed as a machinist alongside white workers.
Location: Schenectady, New York Participants: Union workers at General Electric Company Event date: June 1917 (a few days before June 30, 1917)
Source text
The color issue was created when a young colored man—Wendall King—was put to work in the machine shops of the General Electric Company. King is of a good family in upper Troy, N. Y., and was graduated

p. 1

Machinists at a General Electric plant in Schenectady walked out in protest against discriminatory color line policies, parading from the factory gates to Crescent Park.

Machinists walked out from their workplace in a labor action.
Location: Denver Participants: Machinists Event date: 1917-06-30

1917-06-30 strike
Approximately 2,500 machinists walked out from their workplace, including those working on government contracts.

Source text
There were about 2,500 machinists who left the works this morning, practically all the machinists employed by the company, and many of them were busy on government work. Just what effect, if any, this

p. 1
1917-07-21 delegation
Black residents of Muskogee successfully opposed and defeated a proposed segregation act through collective action.
Location: Muskogee Participants: Black residents/community Event date: nan
Source text
Blacks Defeat Segregation Act in Muskogee
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-07-21 delegation
Colored people of Denver organized through the local business league to oppose a proposed segregation ordinance, successfully defeating it in city council.
Location: Denver Participants: Colored people of Denver, local business league Event date: 1917-07-21
Source text
The proposed segregation ordinance which wnarrow minded unpatriotic white men sought to pass here was defeated by the city council last week after a determined fight made against the nefarious bill by
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 mass_meeting
An editorial call for African Americans to gather collectively from all regions to confront racial oppression and discrimination by white Americans.
Location: nan Participants: African Americans called to gather from east, west, north, and south Event date: nan
Source text
If White Americans during such trying times can cling with such a struggling grip to their color-phobia, should we be blamed for striking a blow for our own continued existence? When the Caucasian, wh
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 mass_meeting
A call for African American racial leaders and masses to unite in response to the East St. Louis riots and systemic racial violence and discrimination.
Location: East St. Louis, Illinois Participants: African American racial leaders and masses Event date: nan
Source text
The party in power has its iron heels on our necks. Our nation's chief executive and his associates in power, has not only done more less for us, but has done more against us, than any other administr
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 mass_meeting
A call issued by the president of the National Equal Rights League for the tenth annual convention and second National Congress of All Colored Americans to convene in September in response to unjust conditions affecting African Americans.
Location: Location not specified, though convention in September implied Participants: National Equal Rights League members and All Colored Americans Event date: September (year of convention, 1917 implied)
Source text
And yet, in view of unjust and unbearable conditions that continue, with increasing horror, to afflict all Colored Americans, we challenge this nation, and the world to show why we have not just and s
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 mass_meeting
A large assembly of African American citizens gathered to address grievances related to national injustice and lack of equal treatment during World War I.
Location: nan Participants: African American citizens, approximately twelve million strong Event date: nan
Source text
Our nation, with the rest of the socalled civilized world, is agonizing in the throes of world-wide "human wreckage." Alarming indeed, therefore, must be the condition of any particular group of Ameri
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 parade
African American men held a silent parade in New York to express grievance and demonstrate collective action.
Location: New York Participants: Race Men (African American men) Event date: 1917
Source text
Race Men Hold Silent Parade in New York.
 Denver Star
p. 1
1917-08-04 parade
A silent parade down Fifth Avenue organized by Negro residents of New York to protest the East St. Louis race riots.
Location: New York, Fifth Avenue Participants: Negro residents of New York (more than 2,000 announced) Event date: July 28, 1917
Source text
New York, July 28.—As a protest against the recent E. St. Louis race riots, Negro residents of New York have arranged for a demonstration today, to take the form of a silent parade down Fifth Avenue.

p. 1

Source text

Source text

1917-08-18 petition

PROTEST TO THE PRESIDENT AGAINST RIOTS AND LYNCH INGS. "SOME PUBLIC WORD WANTED," THAT WOULD GIVE HOPE AND COURAGE TO THE NEGROES.

ments. "We ask, therefore, immediate action by the Congress and the President of the United States." (Signed) Rev. F. A. Cullen, Chairman, James Weldon Johnson. Vice Chairman, Rev. Chas. D. Martin,

p. 1
1918-03-02 delegation
A delegation from the NAACP presented President Wilson with a petition signed by 12,000 citizens requesting executive clemency for five Negro soldiers sentenced to death and a review of cases for 41 others sentenced to life imprisonment from the Houston riot court martial.
Location: Washington, D.C. Participants: James Weldon Johnson (NAACP field secretary), Rev. George Frazier Miller, Rev. Frank M. Hyder, Rev. F. A. Cullen, and President Wilson Event date: 1918-03-02
Source text
PRESIDENT Wilson on Tuesday afternoon received a delegation of four members of the New York branch of the National Association for the Advancement of Colored People by special appointment. The delegat

p. 1
1918-03-02 delegation
A delegation met with the President to present grievances or demands.
Location: nan Participants: A delegation Event date: 1918-03-02
Source text
The President received the delegation very cordially and granted them

p. 1
1918-03-02 delegation
A delegation met with the President to present cases of condemned men and inform him about racial violence incidents including the Estill Springs burning and similar incidents in Memphis and Dyersburg, Tennessee.
Location: Washington, D.C. (implied location of Presidential meeting) Participants: A committee/delegation meeting with the President Event date: nan
Source text

1918-03-02 delegation
A delegation or petition addressed to the President demanding action against racial violence and lynching, specifically referencing the East St. Louis riots and recent lynchings in Tennessee.
Location: East St. Louis, Memphis Tennessee, Dyersburg Tennessee, Estill Springs Tennessee Participants: African American representatives addressing the President Event date: 1918-03-02 (publication date; references events from previous 9 months)
Source text

A delegation of prominent African American leaders met with Assistant Secretary of Labor Post to demand the separation of Negro labor problems into a new bureau headed by a Black official.

Source text

1919-03-22 strike
One hundred and fifty Negro broilermakers at the Henderson Shipbuilding Company walked out in protest after a coworker was discharged for defending himself against a foreman's insults, demanding the worker's reinstatement and the foreman's dismissal.
Location: Mobile, Alabama Participants: 150 Negro broilermakers at Henderson Shipbuilding Company Event date: March 10, 1919
Source text

Source text

1920-01-10 mass_meeting

1929-01-05 mass_meeting
A mass meeting sponsored by the local chamber of commerce where citizens expressed determination to bring justice for a lynching victim, with rewards offered and resolutions adopted calling for action by authorities.

1929-01-05 demonstration
Audience members walked out in protest against W.E.B. Du Bois's remarks criticizing Negro newspapers at an N.A.A.C.P. Founders' Day meeting.
Location: Washington, D.C. Participants: Audience members at the N.A.A.C.P. meeting Event date: December 22, 1928
Source text

1929-02-02 mass_meeting
A mass meeting held to rebuke a lawfully constituted body of men and call for facts regarding a crime.
Location: Cleveland Participants: Leaders and members of the African American community Event date: January 20, 1929
Source text
Somebody blundered in suggesting, nay more, in calling the "Fleming" mass-meeting, last Sunday P.M., Jan. 20th. To me, it was ill-advised and will, if it has not already, prove a miserable fiasco. How

1929-03-02 strike
Over 100 women workers at Bayuk Brothers cigar factory walked out in protest against miserable working conditions and inadequate wages.

Source text

1929-03-16 mass_meeting
A public meeting where speakers demanded justice for the shooting death of Philip Harris and called for the removal of a political leader seen as betraying the African American community.
Location: Cleveland Participants: Rev. Frederick Handy (presiding), multiple speakers, community members Event date: 1929-03-16

1929-03-16 mass_meeting
A mass meeting was held to discuss racial justice issues, police misconduct, and inadequate representation of African Americans in political leadership.
Location: Jersey City, Public School No. 29 Participants: Mrs. Evelyn B. Smith, Bion Jones (Republican leader), and other community residents Event date: nan
Source text
"Many a southerner soffs at the notion of any high moral standards among 'Negro' women. Now I ask you is that the way in which to encourage chastity sufficient to repel the advances of lewd white men?
 Cleveland Gazette
p. 1
1929-03-16 mass_meeting
Over 700 African Americans gathered at a mass meeting to condemn a police system allowing an officer who killed a citizen to remain on duty, and to demand the dismissal of Patrolman Joseph Gantner pending trial.
Location: Jersey City, N.J. Participants: More than 700 African American residents of Jersey City Event date: Friday night (approximately one week before March 16, 1929)
Source text
Seven Hundred at Mass Meeting Deplore Act of One of the Race—Also Insist Patrolman Who Killed Man Be Dismissed Pending Trial. Jersey City, N. J.—A police system which permits a patrolman to carry on
Cleveland Gazette

Dr. John R. Hawkins submitted a memorandum to President Herbert Hoover demanding protection against unfair treatment and equal opportunity for African Americans.

Civil rights activists conducted a sustained segregation fight through lobbying efforts and investigations, presenting their findings to James Weldon Johnson and other NAACP officials.

Editorial campaign against the establishment of separate hospitals for African Americans in Cleveland, advocating for equal access to all city institutions and facilities.

A mass meeting where resolutions were adopted addressing labor discrimination, international grievances, and demanding abolition of forced labor and restoration of autonomy to colonized territories.

1929-07-06 mass_meeting
A mass meeting of 6,000 men and women held in Public Hall where Representative Fish defended Mrs. Oscar De Priest's invitation to tea at the White House and criticized southern political opposition to racial courtesy and equality.
Location: Public Hall Participants: 6,000 men and women, Representative Fish Event date: Sunday afternoon (context: July 6, 1929)

1929-07-13 petition
The New York delegation at the American Federation of Teachers' thirteenth annual convention presented resolutions demanding the abolition of jim-crow schools and equal educational facilities and pay for Black and white teachers.

Source text

1929-10-05 other
Forty-two leading clergymen issued a public statement condemning racial exclusiveness in churches, specifically condemning Rev. Blackshear's exclusion of Negro members from his congregation.
Location: Texas (Rev. Blackshear's church location mentioned) Participants: Forty-two leading clergymen including Dr. Harry Emerson Fosdick, Dr. Henry Sloane Coffin, Dr. George A. Butrick, and Dr. Samuel McRea Cavert Event date: Two Sundays prior to October 5, 1929

1929-11-02 mass_meeting
A mass meeting in Cincinnati where speakers urged African Americans to vote together, support their own candidates, and break down racial barriers in theaters, restaurants, and houses of worship.

1929-11-09 delegation
African American leaders Robert S. Abbott and Paul Robeson lodged formal protests against racial discrimination in England, prompting a Labor MP to raise the issue in Parliament and coordinate with sympathizers to address the color bar.
Location: London, England Participants: Robert S. Abbott, Paul Robeson, James Marley (Labor M.P.), Prime Minister Ramsay MacDonald, Society of Friends (Quakers) Event date: nan
Source text

Public and private appeals made to a labor union to reverse its discriminatory denial of membership to a Black carpet layer.

Public meeting where NAACP officials and city councilmen opposed a proposed segregated hospital branch, advocating instead for integration of existing City Hospital facilities.

Rev. Bailey and colored medical professionals confronted city officials, including Councilman George and City Manager Hopkins, over segregation policies at City Hospital and opposition to the Gregg resolution.
